attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no dumping long raw error logs unless asked — quote shortest decisive line. Standard well-known tech acronyms OK (DB/API/HTTP); never invent new abbreviations (cfg/impl/req/res/fn) — tokenizer split them same as full word: zero token saved, reader still decode. Full word cheaper AND clearer. No causal arrows (→) either — own token, save nothing. Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Preserve user's dominant language. User write Portuguese → reply Portuguese caveman. User write Spanish → reply Spanish caveman. Compress the style, not the language. No forced English openings or status phrases. ALWAYS keep technical terms, code, API names, CLI commands, commit-type keywords (feat/fix/...), and exact error strings verbatim — unless user explicitly ask for translation.

No self-reference. Never name or announce the style. No "caveman mode on", "me caveman think", no third-person caveman tags. Output caveman-only — never normal answer plus "Caveman:" recap. Exception: user explicitly ask what the mode is.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman. No tool-call narration, no decorative tables/emoji, no long raw error-log dumps unless asked. Standard acronyms OK; no invented abbreviations |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman when:
- Security warnings
- Irreversible action confirmations
- Multi-step sequences where fragment order or omitted conjunctions risk misread
- Compression itself creates technical ambiguity (e.g., `"migrate table drop column backup first"` — order unclear without articles/conjunctions)
- User asks to clarify or repeats question

Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

STATUSLINE SETUP NEEDED: The caveman plugin includes a statusline badge showing active mode (e.g. [CAVEMAN], [CAVEMAN:ULTRA]). It is not configured yet. To enable, add this to /Users/kritarth/.claude/settings.json: "statusLine": { "type": "command", "command": "bash \"/Users/kritarth/.claude/plugins/cache/caveman/caveman/0d95a81d35a9/src/hooks/caveman-statusline.sh\"" } Proactively offer to set this up for the user on first interaction.

Currently there is a lot of white space which I don't want. I want it to be perfect 4 pages with all the content completely filled out. mode. If no level specified, use full. addedLines: - caveman:cavecrew-builder: Surgical 1-2 file edit. Typo fixes, single-function rewrites, mechanical renames, comment removal, format-preserving tweaks. Hard refuses 3+ file scope. Returns caveman diff receipt. Use when scope is bounded and obvious; do NOT use for new features, new files (unless asked), or cross-file refactors. (Tools: Read, Edit, Write, Grep, Glob)

- caveman:cavecrew-investigator: Read-only code locator. Returns file:line table for "where is X defined", "what calls Y", "list all uses of Z", "map this directory". Output is caveman-compressed so the main thread eats ~60% fewer tokens than vanilla Explore. Refuses to suggest fixes. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- caveman:cavecrew-reviewer: Diff/branch/file reviewer. One line per finding, severity-tagged, no praise, no scope creep. Output format `path:line: <emoji> <severity>: <problem>. <fix>.` Use for "review this PR", "review my diff", "audit this file". Skips formatting nits unless they change meaning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Bash)
- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

attachment.content:
- caveman:caveman-commit: Generate terse caveman-style commit message
- caveman:caveman-init: Drop the always-on caveman activation rule into the current repo for every IDE agent
- caveman:caveman-review: One-line code review comments
- caveman:caveman-stats: Real session token usage + lifetime savings + USD. Tweetable line via --share.
- caveman:caveman: Switch caveman intensity level (lite/full/ultra/wenyan)
- caveman:cavecrew: Decision guide for delegating to caveman-style subagents. Tells the main thread WHEN to spawn `cavecrew-investigator` (locate code), `cavecrew-builder` (1-2 file edit), or `cavecrew-reviewer` (diff review) instead of doing the work inline or using vanilla `Explore`. Subagent output is caveman-compressed so the tool-result injected back into main context is ~60% smaller — main context lasts longer across long sessions. Trigger: "delegate to subagent", "use cavecrew", "spawn investigator/builder/reviewer", "save context", "compressed agent output".
- caveman:caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts output tokens 65% (measured) by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
- caveman:caveman-compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman-compress FILEPATH or "compress memory file"
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:caveman-stats: Show real token usage and estimated savings for the current session. Reads directly from the Claude Code session log — no AI estimation. Triggers on /caveman-stats. Output is injected by the mode-tracker hook; the model itself does not compute the numbers.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin
- desktop-commander:ai-tools-setup
- desktop-commander:computer-health-check
- desktop-commander:desktop-commander-overview
- desktop-commander:knowledge-base
- desktop-commander:obsidian-vault
- desktop-commander:terminal
- anthropic-skills:academic-humanizer
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx
- anthropic-skills:pdf
- anthropic-skills:pptx
- anthropic-skills:schedule
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator
- anthropic-skills:xlsx
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review
- security-review

59	\title{Separating Safety from Preference: A Role-Gated Evaluation Platform for Clinical Rehabilitation LLMs}
60	
61	% HumanSys uses single-blind review. Verify emails/DOI metadata separately if needed.
62	\author{
63	  \normalfont
64	  Kritarth Dandapat, Tanmay Anishkumar Master, Anarghya Das,
65	  Wei Bo, Mingxi Lei, Lora Cavuoto, Heamchand Subryan, Wenyao Xu \\
66	  \\
67	  University at Buffalo \\
68	}
69	
70	\begin{abstract}
71	Clinical rehabilitation agents need evaluation data that separates
72	preferred answers from clinically safe answers. We present
73	\textsc{WebQA}, a role-gated platform for collecting blinded judgments
74	on rehabilitation LLM responses. WebQA separates domain-expert ratings
75	of safety and correctness from end-user ratings of helpfulness, affect,
76	and clarity, and stores these rubrics beside forced best-to-worst
77	rankings for audit, supervised fine-tuning, and preference learning.
78	
79	In a pilot with 9 raters and 200 rehabilitation questions, WebQA
80	collected 435 complete triple-response judgments and 1{,}305 scored
81	candidate rows. The mRehab Advisory Agent received the most
82	first-place rankings (232/435, 53.3\%; $p < 0.001$ against chance) and
83	was preferred over text-only and tool-using GPT-4.1 mini comparators
84	in pairwise rankings (70.6\% and 61.8\%). Experts, however, marked
85	mRehab responses safe less often than either comparator (88.5\% vs.\
86	94.3\%; $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$). The pilot shows why clinical-agent data
87	collection should preserve both preference and safety signals: the
88	response most often preferred by raters is not automatically the
89	response safest to use for training.
90	\end{abstract}

116	\keywords{human-centered AI, clinical LLM evaluation, rehabilitation,
117	preference elicitation, data collection, human-in-the-loop alignment}
118	
119	\maketitle
120	
121	\section{Introduction}
122	A separate, independently submitted paper introduces a hybrid voice
123	interface for home rehabilitation: a Mobile Action Agent that executes
124	app commands on device, and a cloud Advisory Agent that answers
125	rehabilitation questions using user data, clinical knowledge, and
126	coaching context. That system targets interaction barriers in mRehab,
127	a smartphone-based upper-limb rehabilitation platform previously
128	studied for usability, in-home use, engagement barriers, and
129	sensor-derived outcome
130	prediction~\cite{bhattacharjya2019mrehab,langan2020inhome,%
131	bhattacharjya2021usability,bo2022progressive,bhattacharjya2023engaging}.
132	This self-contained paper asks a complementary question: once such
133	an advisory agent exists, how should developers collect human
134	judgments for both model improvement and clinical audit?
135	
136	The question matters because rehabilitation advice is not a generic
137	chatbot domain. A response can be fluent, encouraging, and preferred
138	by users while still being unsafe, over-specific, or poorly calibrated
139	for pain, fatigue, stopping rules, or exercise progression. Medical
140	LLM evaluations therefore increasingly rely on human rubric
141	judgment~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. For a
142	rehabilitation agent, however, a single preference label is not
143	enough: domain experts and end users should judge different aspects
144	of the same response, and the data export should retain those
145	distinctions for later filtering and training.
146	
147	WebQA is the data collection and evaluation layer used around the mRehab
148	Advisory Agent. It separates rater cohorts, enforces server-side
149	response blinding with stable per-rater display orders, and exports
150	flat audit rows, preferred-response supervised fine-tuning (SFT)
151	examples, and chosen-rejected preference pairs. The output is not only
152	a benchmark score; it is a reusable alignment dataset with provenance.
153	
154	\FloatBarrier
155	\section{Related Work}
156	
157	Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured human
158	judgment rather than automatic
159	metrics~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. LLM-as-judge
160	pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena scale preference
161	collection but substitute a model judgment for a clinician
162	one~\cite{zheng2023judging}; WebQA instead keeps a human rater in the
163	loop with a role-specific rubric, closer to prior rehabilitation
164	conversational-agent evaluations~\cite{hocking2023design}. Its forced
165	ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable
166	than independent Likert ratings~\cite{KiritchenkoM17bestworst}, and the
167	ranked triples double as preference pairs in the style of RLHF and
168	DPO~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}. WebQA also keeps
169	safety rubrics beside the rank, since preference signals alone can
170	reward persuasive but incorrect
171	answers~\cite{sharma2023towards}, and splits expert and end-user
172	cohorts, since agreement should be assessed within, not across,
173	populations with different expertise~\cite{artstein2008inter}.
174	
175	\FloatBarrier
176	\section{WebQA Data Collection Method}
177	
178	General annotation tools can collect ratings, but the mRehab setting
179	required cohort-specific instruments, server-side condition blinding,
180	and exports that preserve safety labels alongside preference ranks.
181	WebQA was built as a role-gated platform with tester, rater, and admin
182	workflows. The present paper analyzes only Rater Bench data collected
183	by the \texttt{rater} cohort, which uses blinded scoring and forced
184	ranking.
185	
186	\begin{figure*}[t]
187	  \centering
188	  \includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{figures/webq_workflow.pdf}
189	  \caption{WebQA workflow for blinded human evaluation and data export.
190	  Panel A: benchmark construction, server-side blinding, cohort-specific
191	  rubric collection, and rank validation. Panel B: flat export and its
192	  four downstream products, aggregate analysis, a preferred-response
193	  SFT set, chosen/rejected preference pairs, and safety filtering.}
194	  \Description{Two-panel flowchart. Panel A shows a benchmark bundle
195	  flowing through server-side blinding and a rater interface, splitting
196	  into domain-expert and end-user rating paths that merge into a
197	  validated submission. Panel B shows a DynamoDB evaluation record
198	  flowing through flat export, which fans out into four downstream
199	  products: aggregate analysis, a preferred-SFT set, preference pairs,
200	  and safety filtering.}
201	  \label{fig:workflow}
202	\end{figure*}
203	
204	Figure~\ref{fig:workflow} summarizes the collection and export
205	workflow. Rater Bench uses fixed benchmark bundles: each item contains
206	a rehabilitation question and exactly three model-generated candidate
207	answers, none manually authored. All three conditions are built on
208	GPT-4.1 mini but differ in context and tool access
209	(Table~\ref{tab:conditions}). The benchmark uses four question
210	categories (Table~\ref{tab:question_categories}), and each rater sees a
211	cohort-specific rubric (Table~\ref{tab:rubric-definitions}).
212	
213	\begin{table}[t]
214	  \footnotesize
215	  \centering
216	  \caption{Rater Bench response conditions (all GPT-4.1 mini).}
217	  \label{tab:conditions}
218	  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.18\linewidth}p{0.74\linewidth}@{}}
219	    \toprule
220	    Condition & Configuration \\
221	    \midrule
222	    mRehab & Advisory Agent with Data Analysis, Health Expert, and Rehab Coaching subagents; subagents retrieve and use patient data. \\
223	    Tool-GPT & GPT-4.1 mini receives only the user query and can call a Python tool that retrieves AWS data. \\
224	    Text-GPT & GPT-4.1 mini receives the user query plus a markdown table of the full user data, with no tool access. \\
225	    \bottomrule
226	  \end{tabular}
227	\end{table}
228	
229	\begin{table}[t]
230	  \centering
231	  \scriptsize
232	  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2.5pt}
233	  \caption{Rater Bench question categories.}
234	  \label{tab:question_categories}
235	  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.16\linewidth}p{0.40\linewidth}p{0.36\linewidth}@{}}
236	    \toprule
237	    Category & Definition & Strong answer should\ldots \\
238	    \midrule
239	    General Knowledge & General info not tied to the user's history. & Give safe, correct guidance; avoid over-personalized claims. \\
240	    Trend & Change over time in user data. & Interpret longitudinally, name direction, avoid unsupported conclusions. \\
241	    Advise & What action to take next. & Combine personalization with caution and stopping rules. \\
242	    Summary & Compact account of recent activity. & Synthesize records accurately for a mobile screen. \\
243	    \bottomrule
244	  \end{tabular}
245	\end{table}
246	
247	\begin{table}[t]
248	  \centering
249	  \scriptsize
250	  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2.5pt}
251	  \caption{Cohort-specific Rater Bench rubric definitions. Clarity (both cohorts) is shown to raters as ``Clarity'' and stored as \texttt{cognitiveness}: is the answer clear, concise, and coherent for a mobile screen?}
252	  \label{tab:rubric-definitions}
253	  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.20\linewidth}p{0.17\linewidth}p{0.10\linewidth}p{0.45\linewidth}@{}}
254	    \toprule
255	    Cohort & Metric & Scale & Definition \\
256	    \midrule
257	    Domain expert & AI Safety & yes/no & Avoids harmful or clinically irresponsible guidance. \\
258	    Domain expert & Correctness & 1--5 & Clinical content is factually accurate and well interpreted. \\
259	    \midrule
260	    End user & Helpfulness & 1--5 & Practically useful for the patient's rehabilitation needs. \\
261	    End user & Affectiveness & 1--5 & Empathetic, encouraging, motivational tone. \\
262	    \bottomrule
263	  \end{tabular}
264	\end{table}
265	
266	For each rater, dataset, and question, the backend computes a stable
267	hashed display order. Raters see only Response A, B, and C; condition
268	keys remain server-side until submission. Domain experts score AI
269	Safety, Correctness, and Clarity. End users score Helpfulness,
270	Affectiveness, and Clarity. All raters also rank the three responses
271	from best to worst, and the backend validates the rank permutation.
272	This design makes each submission reusable as three scored audit rows,
273	one preferred-response SFT example, or chosen-rejected pairs for
274	preference optimization~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}.
275	
276	\FloatBarrier
277	\section{Pilot Results}
278	
279	The pilot should be read as a data-collection and audit study, not as
280	a clinical validation trial. It produced 435 complete judgments and
281	1{,}305 scored candidate rows from 9 raters over 200 questions: 314
282	judgments by domain experts and 121 by end users. The question set
283	covered General Knowledge (182 judgments), Trend (94), Advise (60),
284	and Summary (99). Free-text feedback fields were empty; the scored
285	outputs are rankings and rubric values.
286	
287	\begin{table}[t]
288	  \centering
289	  \footnotesize
290	  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt}
291	  \caption{Pilot results by condition (Table~\ref{tab:conditions}).}
292	  \label{tab:pilot}
293	  \begin{tabular}{lrrrrrr}
294	    \toprule
295	    Condition & 1st & Rank & Safe & E.Clar & U.Aff & U.Clar \\
296	    \midrule
297	    mRehab & 232/435 & 1.676 & 88.5\% & 4.318 & 4.074 & 4.289 \\
298	    Tool-GPT & 133/435 & 2.018 & 94.3\% & 3.930 & 3.124 & 3.967 \\
299	    Text-GPT & 70/435 & 2.306 & 94.3\% & 3.070 & 3.198 & 3.388 \\
300	    \bottomrule
301	  \end{tabular}
302	\end{table}
303	
304	\begin{figure*}[t]
305	  \centering
306	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
307	    \centering
308	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_first_place.pdf}\\[-2pt]
309	    {\footnotesize (a) First-place rankings}
310	  \end{minipage}\hfill
311	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
312	    \centering
313	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_pairwise_preferences.pdf}\\[-2pt]
314	    {\footnotesize (b) Pairwise preferences}
315	  \end{minipage}\\[4pt]
316	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
317	    \centering
318	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_safety_clarity.pdf}\\[-2pt]
319	    {\footnotesize (c) Expert safety and clarity}
320	  \end{minipage}\hfill
321	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
322	    \centering
323	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_category_rankings.pdf}\\[-2pt]
324	    {\footnotesize (d) First-place rankings by category}
325	  \end{minipage}
326	  \caption{Rater Bench result plots. (a) First-place rankings across
327	  435 judgments. (b) Pairwise preferences reconstructed from forced
328	  rankings. (c) Expert safety rates and clarity scores by condition.
329	  (d) First-place rankings by question category.}
330	  \Description{Four-panel figure. (a) Bar chart: mRehab 232 first-place
331	  rankings, Tool-GPT 133, Text-GPT 70. (b) Horizontal bar chart:
332	  mRehab preferred over Text-GPT and Tool-GPT, Tool-GPT preferred over
333	  Text-GPT. (c) Combined bar/line chart: lower expert safety and higher
334	  expert clarity for mRehab. (d) Grouped bar chart: mRehab leading in
335	  General Knowledge, Trend, and Summary, with Advise nearly tied.}
336	  \label{fig:results}
337	\end{figure*}
338	
339	Table~\ref{tab:pilot} summarizes the main signals. The mRehab
340	Advisory Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
341	53.3\%), above the 33.3\% chance level by a one-sided binomial test
342	($p < 0.001$). Pairwise preferences reconstructed from the ranked
343	triples also favored mRehab: 307/435 comparisons over text-only
344	GPT-4.1 mini (70.6\%, $p < 0.001$) and 269/435 over tool-using
345	GPT-4.1 mini (61.8\%, $p < 0.001$). Tool-using GPT-4.1 mini was
346	preferred over text-only GPT-4.1 mini in 261/435 comparisons (60.0\%).
347	Figure~\ref{fig:results} shows the
348	same pattern across first-place, pairwise, rubric, and category views.
349	These tests treat each of the 435 judgments as independent, but the
350	same 9 raters and 200 questions recur across conditions, so judgments
351	are clustered by rater and by item; the reported $p$-values should be
352	read as descriptive rather than confirmatory pending a clustered or
353	mixed-effects reanalysis.
354	
355	The same dataset also surfaces a safety issue that a preference-only
356	pipeline would miss. Experts marked mRehab responses safe in 88.5\%
357	of scored rows, compared with 94.3\% for each comparator (two-proportion
358	$z$-test, $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$, subject to the same clustering caveat).
359	The condition most often preferred by raters was also the condition
360	with the lowest expert safety rate.
361	Category-level rankings point to where more data are needed: mRehab
362	led in General Knowledge (102/182, 56.0\%), Summary (59/99, 59.6\%),
363	and Trend (48/94, 51.1\%), but Advise was nearly tied (23/60, 38.3\%
364	for mRehab vs.\ 22/60, 36.7\% for tool-using GPT-4.1 mini). Advice
365	items combine clinical caution, personalization, and action guidance,
366	making them the highest-priority category for expanded safety review.
367	
368	\FloatBarrier
369	\section{Alignment Use}
370	
371	WebQA turns evaluation into an alignment data pipeline. A rank-only
372	export would select mRehab responses for SFT at the highest rate and
373	hide the lower safety rate. WebQA instead stores rank, cohort, blind
374	order, condition key, response text, prompt text, and rubric values
375	together, so developers can build preferred-response SFT examples and
376	preference pairs while filtering out examples whose safety,
377	correctness, or clarity falls below a threshold.
378	
379	The pilot also clarifies the division of labor with the separately
380	submitted mRehab agent-architecture paper, which evaluates command
381	execution, intent routing, and response quality: WebQA evaluates the
382	collection process that makes those outputs usable for iterative
383	improvement. Future deployments can extend the same data model from
384	fixed Rater Bench items to multi-turn chat logs and workflow traces.
385	
386	\FloatBarrier
387	\section{Discussion and Conclusion}
388	
389	The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced, free-text
390	feedback was empty, we do not yet report inter-rater reliability, and
391	the significance tests in Section~4 do not yet account for clustering
392	by rater and by question, so they should be treated as descriptive.
393	Interactive chat-study data has not been collected, so multi-turn
394	safety and workflow-trace usefulness remain open questions. The next
395	collection should balance assignments across cohorts, require short
396	rationales for low ratings, report within-cohort agreement, and rerun
397	condition comparisons with rater- and question-level random effects.
398	
399	Despite these limits, WebQA makes a concrete methodological point for
400	clinical agent development: human alignment data should not collapse
401	judgment to a single winner. Separating rater cohorts, retaining
402	safety labels beside preference ranks, and exporting provenance-rich
403	records turns a human evaluation study into a reusable data layer for
404	auditing, filtering, and improving rehabilitation agents.
405	
406	\section*{Ethics Statement}
407	Data collection was conducted under University at Buffalo IRB protocol
408	\textbf{[IRB PROTOCOL NUMBER]}. All 9 raters gave informed consent;
409	questions/responses were synthetic and de-identified, and response
410	identity was server-side blinded throughout.
411

\begin{document}

\title{Separating Safety from Preference: A Role-Gated Evaluation Platform for Clinical Rehabilitation LLMs}

% HumanSys uses single-blind review. Verify emails/DOI metadata separately if needed.
\author{
  \normalfont
  Kritarth Dandapat, Tanmay Anishkumar Master, Anarghya Das,
  Wei Bo, Mingxi Lei, Lora Cavuoto, Heamchand Subryan, Wenyao Xu \\
  \\
  University at Buffalo \\
}

\begin{abstract}
Clinical rehabilitation agents need evaluation data that separates
preferred answers from clinically safe answers. We present
\textsc{WebQA}, a role-gated platform for collecting blinded judgments
on rehabilitation LLM responses. WebQA separates domain-expert ratings
of safety and correctness from end-user ratings of helpfulness, affect,
and clarity, and stores these rubrics beside forced best-to-worst
rankings for audit, supervised fine-tuning, and preference learning.

In a pilot with 9 raters and 200 rehabilitation questions, WebQA
collected 435 complete triple-response judgments and 1{,}305 scored
candidate rows. The mRehab Advisory Agent received the most
first-place rankings (232/435, 53.3\%; $p < 0.001$ against chance) and
was preferred over text-only and tool-using GPT-4.1 mini comparators
in pairwise rankings (70.6\% and 61.8\%). Experts, however, marked
mRehab responses safe less often than either comparator (88.5\% vs.\
94.3\%; $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$). The pilot shows why clinical-agent data
collection should preserve both preference and safety signals: the
response most often preferred by raters is not automatically the
response safest to use for training.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
A separate, independently submitted paper introduces a hybrid voice
interface for home rehabilitation: a Mobile Action Agent that executes
app commands on device, and a cloud Advisory Agent that answers
rehabilitation questions using user data, clinical knowledge, and
coaching context. That system targets interaction barriers in mRehab,
a smartphone-based upper-limb rehabilitation platform previously
studied for usability, in-home use, engagement barriers, and
sensor-derived outcome
prediction~\cite{bhattacharjya2019mrehab,langan2020inhome,%
bhattacharjya2021usability,bo2022progressive,bhattacharjya2023engaging}.
This self-contained paper asks a complementary question: once such
an advisory agent exists, how should developers collect human
judgments for both model improvement and clinical audit?

The question matters because rehabilitation advice is not a generic
chatbot domain. A response can be fluent, encouraging, and preferred
by users while still being unsafe, over-specific, or poorly calibrated
for pain, fatigue, stopping rules, or exercise progression. Medical
LLM evaluations therefore increasingly rely on human rubric
judgment~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. For a
rehabilitation agent, however, a single preference label is not
enough: domain experts and end users should judge different aspects
of the same response, and the data export should retain those
distinctions for later filtering and training.

WebQA is the data collection and evaluation layer used around the mRehab
Advisory Agent. It separates rater cohorts, enforces server-side
response blinding with stable per-rater display orders, and exports
flat audit rows, preferred-response supervised fine-tuning (SFT)
examples, and chosen-rejected preference pairs. The output is not only
a benchmark score; it is a reusable alignment dataset with provenance.

\FloatBarrier
\section{Related Work}

Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured human
judgment rather than automatic
metrics~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. LLM-as-judge
pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena scale preference
collection but substitute a model judgment for a clinician
one~\cite{zheng2023judging}; WebQA instead keeps a human rater in the
loop with a role-specific rubric, closer to prior rehabilitation
conversational-agent evaluations~\cite{hocking2023design}. Its forced
ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable
than independent Likert ratings~\cite{KiritchenkoM17bestworst}, and the
ranked triples double as preference pairs in the style of RLHF and
DPO~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}. WebQA also keeps
safety rubrics beside the rank, since preference signals alone can
reward persuasive but incorrect
answers~\cite{sharma2023towards}, and splits expert and end-user
cohorts, since agreement should be assessed within, not across,
populations with different expertise~\cite{artstein2008inter}.

\FloatBarrier
\section{WebQA Data Collection Method}

General annotation tools can collect ratings, but the mRehab setting
required cohort-specific instruments, server-side condition blinding,
and exports that preserve safety labels alongside preference ranks.
WebQA was built as a role-gated platform with tester, rater, and admin
workflows. The present paper analyzes only Rater Bench data collected
by the \texttt{rater} cohort, which uses blinded scoring and forced
ranking.

\begin{figure*}[t]

Figure~\ref{fig:workflow} summarizes the collection and export

    Trend & Change over time in user data. & Interpret longitudinally, name direction, avoid unsupported conclusions. \\
    Advise & What action to take next. & Combine personalization with caution and stopping rules. \\
    Summary & Compact account of recent activity. & Synthesize records accurately for a mobile screen. \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \scriptsize
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2.5pt}
  \caption{Cohort-specific Rater Bench rubric definitions. Clarity (both cohorts) is shown to raters as ``Clarity'' and stored as \texttt{cognitiveness}: is the answer clear, concise, and coherent for a mobile screen?}
  \label{tab:rubric-definitions}
  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.20\linewidth}p{0.17\linewidth}p{0.10\linewidth}p{0.45\linewidth}@{}}
    \toprule
    Cohort & Metric & Scale & Definition \\
    \midrule
    Domain expert & AI Safety & yes/no & Avoids harmful or clinically irresponsible guidance. \\
    Domain expert & Correctness & 1--5 & Clinical content is factually accurate and well interpreted. \\
    \midrule
    End user & Helpfulness & 1--5 & Practically useful for the patient's rehabilitation needs. \\
    End user & Affectiveness & 1--5 & Empathetic, encouraging, motivational tone. \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

For each rater, dataset, and question, the backend computes a stable
hashed display order. Raters see only Response A, B, and C; condition
keys remain server-side until submission. Domain experts score AI
Safety, Correctness, and Clarity. End users score Helpfulness,
Affectiveness, and Clarity. All raters also rank the three responses
from best to worst, and the backend validates the rank permutation.
This design makes each submission reusable as three scored audit rows,
one preferred-response SFT example, or chosen-rejected pairs for
preference optimization~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}.

\FloatBarrier
\section{Pilot Results}

The pilot should be read as a data-collection and audit study, not as
a clinical validation trial. It produced 435 complete judgments and
1{,}305 scored candidate rows from 9 raters over 200 questions: 314
judgments by domain experts and 121 by end users. The question set
covered General Knowledge (182 judgments), Trend (94), Advise (60),
and Summary (99). Free-text feedback fields were empty; the scored
outputs are rankings and rubric values.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \footnotesize
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt}
  \caption{Pilot results by condition (Table~\ref{tab:conditions}).}
  \label{tab:pilot}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrrrrr}
    \toprule
    Condition & 1st & Rank & Safe & E.Clar & U.Aff & U.Clar \\
    \midrule
    mRehab & 232/435 & 1.676 & 88.5\% & 4.318 & 4.074 & 4.289 \\
    Tool-GPT & 133/435 & 2.018 & 94.3\% & 3.930 & 3.124 & 3.967 \\
    Text-GPT & 70/435 & 2.306 & 94.3\% & 3.070 & 3.198 & 3.388 \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_first_place.pdf}\\[-2pt]
    {\footnotesize (a) First-place rankings}
  \end{minipage}\hfill
  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_pairwise_preferences.pdf}\\[-2pt]
    {\footnotesize (b) Pairwise preferences}
  \end{minipage}\\[4pt]
  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_safety_clarity.pdf}\\[-2pt]
    {\footnotesize (c) Expert safety and clarity}
  \end{minipage}\hfill
  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_category_rankings.pdf}\\[-2pt]
    {\footnotesize (d) First-place rankings by category}
  \end{minipage}
  \caption{Rater Bench result plots. (a) First-place rankings across
  435 judgments. (b) Pairwise preferences reconstructed from forced
  rankings. (c) Expert safety rates and clarity scores by condition.
  (d) First-place rankings by question category.}
  \Description{Four-panel figure. (a) Bar chart: mRehab 232 first-place
  rankings, Tool-GPT 133, Text-GPT 70. (b) Horizontal bar chart:
  mRehab preferred over Text-GPT and Tool-GPT, Tool-GPT preferred over
  Text-GPT. (c) Combined bar/line chart: lower expert safety and higher
  expert clarity for mRehab. (d) Grouped bar chart: mRehab leading in
  General Knowledge, Trend, and Summary, with Advise nearly tied.}
  \label{fig:results}
\end{figure*}

Table~\ref{tab:pilot} summarizes the main signals. The mRehab
Advisory Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
53.3\%), above the 33.3\% chance level by a one-sided binomial test
($p < 0.001$). Pairwise preferences reconstructed from the ranked
triples also favored mRehab: 307/435 comparisons over text-only
GPT-4.1 mini (70.6\%, $p < 0.001$) and 269/435 over tool-using
GPT-4.1 mini (61.8\%, $p < 0.001$). Tool-using GPT-4.1 mini was
preferred over text-only GPT-4.1 mini in 261/435 comparisons (60.0\%).
Figure~\ref{fig:results} shows the
same pattern across first-place, pairwise, rubric, and category views.
These tests treat each of the 435 judgments as independent, but the
same 9 raters and 200 questions recur across conditions, so judgments
are clustered by rater and by item; the reported $p$-values should be
read as descriptive rather than confirmatory pending a clustered or
mixed-effects reanalysis.

The same dataset also surfaces a safety issue that a preference-only
pipeline would miss. Experts marked mRehab responses safe in 88.5\%
of scored rows, compared with 94.3\% for each comparator (two-proportion
$z$-test, $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$, subject to the same clustering caveat).
The condition most often preferred by raters was also the condition
with the lowest expert safety rate.
Category-level rankings point to where more data are needed: mRehab
led in General Knowledge (102/182, 56.0\%), Summary (59/99, 59.6\%),
and Trend (48/94, 51.1\%), but Advise was nearly tied (23/60, 38.3\%
for mRehab vs.\ 22/60, 36.7\% for tool-using GPT-4.1 mini). Advice
items combine clinical caution, personalization, and action guidance,
making them the highest-priority category for expanded safety review.

\FloatBarrier
\section{Alignment Use}

WebQA turns evaluation into an alignment data pipeline. A rank-only
export would select mRehab responses for SFT at the highest rate and
hide the lower safety rate. WebQA instead stores rank, cohort, blind
order, condition key, response text, prompt text, and rubric values
together, so developers can build preferred-response SFT examples and
preference pairs while filtering out examples whose safety,
correctness, or clarity falls below a threshold.

The pilot also clarifies the division of labor with the separately
submitted mRehab agent-architecture paper, which evaluates command
execution, intent routing, and response quality: WebQA evaluates the
collection process that makes those outputs usable for iterative
improvement. Future deployments can extend the same data model from
fixed Rater Bench items to multi-turn chat logs and workflow traces.

\FloatBarrier
\section{Discussion and Conclusion}

The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced, free-text
feedback was empty, we do not yet report inter-rater reliability, and
the significance tests in Section~4 do not yet account for clustering
by rater and by question, so they should be treated as descriptive.
Interactive chat-study data has not been collected, so multi-turn
safety and workflow-trace usefulness remain open questions. The next
collection should balance assignments across cohorts, require short
rationales for low ratings, report within-cohort agreement, and rerun
condition comparisons with rater- and question-level random effects.

Despite these limits, WebQA makes a concrete methodological point for
clinical agent development: human alignment data should not collapse
judgment to a single winner. Separating rater cohorts, retaining
safety labels beside preference ranks, and exporting provenance-rich
records turns a human evaluation study into a reusable data layer for
auditing, filtering, and improving rehabilitation agents.

\section*{Ethics Statement}
Data collection was conducted under University at Buffalo IRB protocol
\textbf{[IRB PROTOCOL NUMBER]}. All 9 raters gave informed consent;
questions/responses were synthetic and de-identified, and response
identity was server-side blinded throughout.

\footnotesize
\bibliography{references}

\end{document}

content.content:
Separating Safety from Preference: A Role-Gated
Evaluation Platform for Clinical Rehabilitation LLMs
Kritarth Dandapat, Tanmay Anishkumar Master, Anarghya Das, Wei Bo, Mingxi
Lei, Lora Cavuoto, Heamchand Subryan, Wenyao Xu
University at Buffalo
ABSTRACT
Clinical rehabilitation agents need evaluation data that
separates preferred answers from clinically safe answers.
We present WebQA, a role-gated platform for collecting blinded judgments on rehabilitation LLM responses.
WebQA separates domain-expert ratings of safety and correctness from end-user ratings of helpfulness, affect, and
clarity, and stores these rubrics beside forced best-to-worst
rankings for audit, supervised fine-tuning, and preference
learning.
In a pilot with 9 raters and 200 rehabilitation questions,
WebQA collected 435 complete triple-response judgments
and 1,305 scored candidate rows. The mRehab Advisory
Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
53.3%; 𝑝 < 0.001 against chance) and was preferred over
text-only and tool-using GPT-4.1 mini comparators in pairwise rankings (70.6% and 61.8%). Experts, however, marked
mRehab responses safe less often than either comparator
(88.5% vs. 94.3%; 𝑧 = −2.59, 𝑝 = 0.005). The pilot shows
why clinical-agent data collection should preserve both
preference and safety signals: the response most often
preferred by raters is not automatically the response safest
to use for training.

KEYWORDS
human-centered AI, clinical LLM evaluation, rehabilitation,
preference elicitation, data collection, human-in-the-loop
alignment

1

INTRODUCTION

A separate, independently submitted paper introduces a
hybrid voice interface for home rehabilitation: a Mobile
Action Agent that executes app commands on device,
and a cloud Advisory Agent that answers rehabilitation
questions using user data, clinical knowledge, and coaching
context. That system targets interaction barriers in mRehab,
a smartphone-based upper-limb rehabilitation platform
previously studied for usability, in-home use, engagement
barriers, and sensor-derived outcome prediction [3–6, 9].
This self-contained paper asks a complementary question:
once such an advisory agent exists, how should developers

collect human judgments for both model improvement and
clinical audit?
The question matters because rehabilitation advice is
not a generic chatbot domain. A response can be fluent,
encouraging, and preferred by users while still being unsafe, over-specific, or poorly calibrated for pain, fatigue,
stopping rules, or exercise progression. Medical LLM
evaluations therefore increasingly rely on human rubric
judgment [1, 13]. For a rehabilitation agent, however, a single preference label is not enough: domain experts and end
users should judge different aspects of the same response,
and the data export should retain those distinctions for
later filtering and training.
WebQA is the data collection and evaluation layer used
around the mRehab Advisory Agent. It separates rater
cohorts, enforces server-side response blinding with stable per-rater display orders, and exports flat audit rows,
preferred-response supervised fine-tuning (SFT) examples,
and chosen-rejected preference pairs. The output is not
only a benchmark score; it is a reusable alignment dataset
with provenance.

2

RELATED WORK

Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured
human judgment rather than automatic metrics [1, 13].
LLM-as-judge pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot
Arena scale preference collection but substitute a model
judgment for a clinician one [14]; WebQA instead keeps a
human rater in the loop with a role-specific rubric, closer
to prior rehabilitation conversational-agent evaluations [7].
Its forced ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable than independent Likert ratings [8],
and the ranked triples double as preference pairs in the
style of RLHF and DPO [10, 11]. WebQA also keeps safety
rubrics beside the rank, since preference signals alone can
reward persuasive but incorrect answers [12], and splits
expert and end-user cohorts, since agreement should be
assessed within, not across, populations with different
expertise [2].

University at Buffalo

mini).

input.old_string:
\caption{WebQA workflow for blinded human evaluation and data export.
  Panel A: benchmark construction, server-side blinding, cohort-specific
  rubric collection, and rank validation. Panel B: flat export and its
  four downstream products, aggregate analysis, a preferred-response
  SFT set, chosen/rejected preference pairs, and safety filtering.}
  \Description{Two-panel flowchart. Panel A shows a benchmark bundle
  flowing through server-side blinding and a rater interface, splitting
  into domain-expert and end-user rating paths that merge into a
  validated submission. Panel B shows a DynamoDB evaluation record
  flowing through flat export, which fans out into four downstream
  products: aggregate analysis, a preferred-SFT set, preference pairs,
  and safety filtering.}
  \label{fig:workflow}
\end{figure*}

6 Discussion and Conclusion
The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced,
free-text feedback was empty, we do not yet report interrater reliability, and the significance tests in Section 4 do not
yet account for clustering by rater and by question, so they
should be treated as descriptive. Interactive chat-study data
has not been collected, so multi-turn safety and workflowtrace usefulness remain open questions. The next collection
should balance assignments across cohorts, require short
rationales for low ratings, report within-cohort agreement,
and rerun condition comparisons with rater- and questionlevel random effects.
Despite these limits, WebQA makes a concrete methodological point for clinical agent development: human alignment data should not collapse judgment to a single winner.

input.old_string:
\begin{abstract}
Clinical rehabilitation agents need evaluation data that separates
preferred answers from clinically safe answers. We present
\textsc{WebQA}, a role-gated platform for collecting blinded judgments
on rehabilitation LLM responses. WebQA separates domain-expert ratings
of safety and correctness from end-user ratings of helpfulness, affect,
and clarity, and stores these rubrics beside forced best-to-worst
rankings for audit, supervised fine-tuning, and preference learning.

In a pilot with 9 raters and 200 rehabilitation questions, WebQA
collected 435 complete triple-response judgments and 1{,}305 scored
candidate rows. The mRehab Advisory Agent received the most
first-place rankings (232/435, 53.3\%; $p < 0.001$ against chance) and
was preferred over text-only and tool-using GPT-4.1 mini comparators
in pairwise rankings (70.6\% and 61.8\%). Experts, however, marked
mRehab responses safe less often than either comparator (88.5\% vs.\
94.3\%; $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$). The pilot shows why clinical-agent data
collection should preserve both preference and safety signals: the
response most often preferred by raters is not automatically the
response safest to use for training.
\end{abstract}

340	  mRehab preferred over Text-GPT and Tool-GPT, Tool-GPT preferred over
341	  Text-GPT. (c) Combined bar/line chart: lower expert safety and higher
342	  expert clarity for mRehab. (d) Grouped bar chart: mRehab leading in
343	  General Knowledge, Trend, and Summary, with Advise nearly tied.}
344	  \label{fig:results}
345	  \vspace{-2pt}
346	\end{figure*}
347	
348	Table~\ref{tab:pilot} summarizes the main signals. The mRehab
349	Advisory Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
350	53.3\%), above the 33.3\% chance level by a one-sided binomial test
351	($p < 0.001$). Pairwise preferences reconstructed from the ranked
352	triples also favored mRehab: 307/435 comparisons over text-only
353	GPT-4.1 mini (70.6\%, $p < 0.001$) and 269/435 over tool-using
354	GPT-4.1 mini (61.8\%, $p < 0.001$). Tool-using GPT-4.1 mini was
355	preferred over text-only GPT-4.1 mini in 261/435 comparisons (60.0\%).
356	Figure~\ref{fig:results} shows the
357	same pattern across first-place, pairwise, rubric, and category views.
358	These tests treat each of the 435 judgments as independent, but the
359	same 9 raters and 200 questions recur across conditions, so judgments
360	are clustered by rater and by item; the reported $p$-values should be
361	read as descriptive rather than confirmatory pending a clustered or
362	mixed-effects reanalysis.
363	
364	The same dataset also surfaces a safety issue that a preference-only
365	pipeline would miss. Experts marked mRehab responses safe in 88.5\%
366	of scored rows, compared with 94.3\% for each comparator (two-proportion
367	$z$-test, $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$, subject to the same clustering caveat).
368	The condition most often preferred by raters was also the condition
369	with the lowest expert safety rate.
370	Category-level rankings point to where more data are needed: mRehab
371	led in General Knowledge (102/182, 56.0\%), Summary (59/99, 59.6\%),
372	and Trend (48/94, 51.1\%), but Advise was nearly tied (23/60, 38.3\%
373	for mRehab vs.\ 22/60, 36.7\% for tool-using GPT-4.1 mini). Advice
374	items combine clinical caution, personalization, and action guidance,
375	making them the highest-priority category for expanded safety review.
376	
377	\section{Alignment Use}
378	
379	WebQA turns evaluation into an alignment data pipeline. A rank-only
380	export would select mRehab responses for SFT at the highest rate and
381	hide the lower safety rate. WebQA instead stores rank, cohort, blind
382	order, condition key, response text, prompt text, and rubric values
383	together, so developers can build preferred-response SFT examples and
384	preference pairs while filtering out examples whose safety,
385	correctness, or clarity falls below a threshold.
386	
387	The pilot also clarifies the division of labor with the separately
388	submitted mRehab agent-architecture paper, which evaluates command
389	execution, intent routing, and response quality: WebQA evaluates the
390	collection process that makes those outputs usable for iterative
391	improvement. Future deployments can extend the same data model from
392	fixed Rater Bench items to multi-turn chat logs and workflow traces.
393	
394	\section{Discussion and Conclusion}
395	
396	The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced, free-text
397	feedback was empty, we do not yet report inter-rater reliability, and
398	the significance tests in Section~4 do not yet account for clustering
399	by rater and by question, so they should be treated as descriptive.
400	Interactive chat-study data has not been collected, so multi-turn
401	safety and workflow-trace usefulness remain open questions. The next
402	collection should balance assignments across cohorts, require short
403	rationales for low ratings, report within-cohort agreement, and rerun
404	condition comparisons with rater- and question-level random effects.
405	
406	Despite these limits, WebQA makes a concrete methodological point for
407	clinical agent development: human alignment data should not collapse
408	judgment to a single winner. Separating rater cohorts, retaining
409	safety labels beside preference ranks, and exporting provenance-rich
410	records turns a human evaluation study into a reusable data layer for
411	auditing, filtering, and improving rehabilitation agents.
412	
413	\section*{Ethics Statement}
414	\vspace{-6pt}
415	Data collection was conducted under University at Buffalo IRB protocol
416	\textbf{[IRB PROTOCOL NUMBER]}. All 9 raters gave informed consent;
417	questions/responses were synthetic and de-identified, and response
418	identity was server-side blinded throughout.
419

\end{figure*}

Table~\ref{tab:pilot} summarizes the main signals. The mRehab
Advisory Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
53.3\%), above the 33.3\% chance level by a one-sided binomial test
($p < 0.001$). Pairwise preferences reconstructed from the ranked
triples also favored mRehab: 307/435 comparisons over text-only
GPT-4.1 mini (70.6\%, $p < 0.001$) and 269/435 over tool-using
GPT-4.1 mini (61.8\%, $p < 0.001$). Tool-using GPT-4.1 mini was
preferred over text-only GPT-4.1 mini in 261/435 comparisons (60.0\%).
Figure~\ref{fig:results} shows the
same pattern across first-place, pairwise, rubric, and category views.
These tests treat each of the 435 judgments as independent, but the
same 9 raters and 200 questions recur across conditions, so judgments
are clustered by rater and by item; the reported $p$-values should be
read as descriptive rather than confirmatory pending a clustered or
mixed-effects reanalysis.

The same dataset also surfaces a safety issue that a preference-only
pipeline would miss. Experts marked mRehab responses safe in 88.5\%
of scored rows, compared with 94.3\% for each comparator (two-proportion
$z$-test, $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$, subject to the same clustering caveat).
The condition most often preferred by raters was also the condition
with the lowest expert safety rate.
Category-level rankings point to where more data are needed: mRehab
led in General Knowledge (102/182, 56.0\%), Summary (59/99, 59.6\%),
and Trend (48/94, 51.1\%), but Advise was nearly tied (23/60, 38.3\%
for mRehab vs.\ 22/60, 36.7\% for tool-using GPT-4.1 mini). Advice
items combine clinical caution, personalization, and action guidance,
making them the highest-priority category for expanded safety review.

\section{Alignment Use}

WebQA turns evaluation into an alignment data pipeline. A rank-only
export would select mRehab responses for SFT at the highest rate and
hide the lower safety rate. WebQA instead stores rank, cohort, blind
order, condition key, response text, prompt text, and rubric values
together, so developers can build preferred-response SFT examples and
preference pairs while filtering out examples whose safety,
correctness, or clarity falls below a threshold.

The pilot also clarifies the division of labor with the separately
submitted mRehab agent-architecture paper, which evaluates command
execution, intent routing, and response quality: WebQA evaluates the
collection process that makes those outputs usable for iterative
improvement. Future deployments can extend the same data model from
fixed Rater Bench items to multi-turn chat logs and workflow traces.

\section{Discussion and Conclusion}

The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced, free-text
feedback was empty, we do not yet report inter-rater reliability, and
the significance tests in Section~4 do not yet account for clustering
by rater and by question, so they should be treated as descriptive.
Interactive chat-study data has not been collected, so multi-turn
safety and workflow-trace usefulness remain open questions. The next
collection should balance assignments across cohorts, require short
rationales for low ratings, report within-cohort agreement, and rerun
condition comparisons with rater- and question-level random effects.

Despite these limits, WebQA makes a concrete methodological point for
clinical agent development: human alignment data should not collapse
judgment to a single winner. Separating rater cohorts, retaining
safety labels beside preference ranks, and exporting provenance-rich
records turns a human evaluation study into a reusable data layer for
auditing, filtering, and improving rehabilitation agents.

\section*{Ethics Statement}
\vspace{-6pt}
Data collection was conducted under University at Buffalo IRB protocol
\textbf{[IRB PROTOCOL NUMBER]}. All 9 raters gave informed consent;
questions/responses were synthetic and de-identified, and response
identity was server-side blinded throughout.

To get Paper 1 (`mRehab_Paper__webQA_.pdf`) safely past the HumanSys workshop reviewers, your structure needs to shift from an "AI/NLP annotation project" to a **deployment and operational experience paper**.

Because the workshop focuses on the implementation and operation of technologies for human-centered applications, you must highlight the platform's infrastructure, workflow mechanics, and the data architecture that connects human experts to AI loops.

Here is a recommended 4-page structural blueprint designed to maximize its fit for the track:

---

## 📄 Proposed Structural Blueprint

### 1. Introduction (approx. 0.5 – 0.75 pages)

* **The Hook:** Frame the problem around the operational hurdles of deploying conversational AI in clinical settings. Explain that generic chat interfaces lack mechanisms to separate what users *like* (preference) from what is clinically responsible (safety).


* **The Pivot:** Introduce WebQA not just as an interface, but as a role-gated, operational data pipeline deployed to manage and audit this specific vulnerability.


* **Strategic Fix:** Remove or soften the phrase *"A separate, independently submitted paper introduces..."*. Make WebQA sound like a fully realized, standalone operational ecosystem infrastructure.



### 2. System Architecture & Implementation (approx. 1.0 page)

* **Role-Gated Infrastructure:** Detail the engineering of the platform's workflows (Admin, Tester, and Rater benches).


* **Blinding & Randomization Engines:** Explain the technical implementation of the server-side stable hashing mechanism that ensures double-blinded presentation orders.


* **Data Models & Schema:** Walk through Panel B of Figure 1. Describe how data flows from live DynamoDB instances into flat export rows that simultaneously serve as Auditing logs, SFT training sets, and DPO preference pairs.



### 3. Operational Deployment & Rubric Design (approx. 0.75 pages)

* **Cohort Segmentation:** Justify the operational choice to separate domain experts (clinicians) from end-users (patients/advocates).


* **Sensing/Modeling Alignment:** Map how the question categories (Trend, Summary, Advise) relate to user histories and data logs generated by health platforms.


* **The Interface Experience:** Briefly describe the design choices made to keep the tool efficient for busy clinicians (e.g., forced rankings over tedious Likert scales).



### 4. Deployment Results & Data Insights (approx. 1.0 page)

* **Pilot Scale:** Report the metrics from the 9-rater, 200-question operational deployment (yielding 435 complete judgments and 1,305 candidate rows).


* **The Core Technical Tension:** Highlight Table 4 and Figure 2. Show the exact systemic conflict: the mRehab configuration won the preference ranking (53.3%) but dropped significantly in expert safety (88.5% vs. 94.3% for comparators).


* **Infrastructure Justification:** Use this tension to prove why the *system design* works. If WebQA hadn't separated these data signals architecturally, the safety flaw would have been baked straight into future training loops.



### 5. Discussion & Deployment Lessons (approx. 0.5 pages)

* **Addressing Limitations Transparently:** Own the lack of inter-rater reliability (IRR) metrics and the descriptive clustering limitations in the current statistical pipeline. Present these not as failures, but as targeted iterations for the next software release.


* **HumanSys Takeaway:** Conclude by reiterating that human-in-the-loop alignment platforms for clinical applications must prioritize rich metadata provenance over simple win/loss tallies.



---

## 🛠️ Formatting & Compression Strategies

* **Shrink the Figure Footprint:** Figure 1 and Figure 2 are quite large in the current layout. Condense Figure 2's four subplots into a tighter $2 \times 2$ grid or merge Table 4's metrics directly into the text to win back valuable page real estate.


* **Consolidate Tables:** Table 1, Table 2, and Table 3 can potentially be compressed or stylized into dense Markdown lists to prevent text fragmentation.


* **Keep Mathematics Textual:** Keep the standard notations (like $p < 0.001$, $z = -2.59$) strictly inline rather than utilizing display mode blocks, ensuring your prose flows efficiently without unnecessary vertical line spacing.

\keywords{human-centered AI, clinical LLM evaluation, rehabilitation,
preference elicitation, data collection, human-in-the-loop alignment}

\maketitle

\section{Introduction}
A separate, independently submitted paper introduces a hybrid voice
interface for home rehabilitation: a Mobile Action Agent that executes
app commands on device, and a cloud Advisory Agent that answers
rehabilitation questions using user data, clinical knowledge, and
coaching context. That system targets interaction barriers in mRehab,
a smartphone-based upper-limb rehabilitation platform previously
studied for usability, in-home use, engagement barriers, and
sensor-derived outcome
prediction~\cite{bhattacharjya2019mrehab,langan2020inhome,%
bhattacharjya2021usability,bo2022progressive,bhattacharjya2023engaging}.
This self-contained paper asks a complementary question: once such
an advisory agent exists, how should developers collect human
judgments for both model improvement and clinical audit?

The question matters because rehabilitation advice is not a generic
chatbot domain. A response can be fluent, encouraging, and preferred
by users while still being unsafe, over-specific, or poorly calibrated
for pain, fatigue, stopping rules, or exercise progression. Medical
LLM evaluations therefore increasingly rely on human rubric
judgment~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. For a
rehabilitation agent, however, a single preference label is not
enough: domain experts and end users should judge different aspects
of the same response, and the data export should retain those
distinctions for later filtering and training.

WebQA is the data collection and evaluation layer used around the mRehab
Advisory Agent. It separates rater cohorts, enforces server-side
response blinding with stable per-rater display orders, and exports
flat audit rows, preferred-response supervised fine-tuning (SFT)
examples, and chosen-rejected preference pairs. The output is not only
a benchmark score; it is a reusable alignment dataset with provenance.

\section{Related Work}

Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured human
judgment rather than automatic
metrics~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. LLM-as-judge
pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena scale preference
collection but substitute a model judgment for a clinician
one~\cite{zheng2023judging}; WebQA instead keeps a human rater in the
loop with a role-specific rubric, closer to prior rehabilitation
conversational-agent evaluations~\cite{hocking2023design}. Its forced
ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable

input.new_string:
\section{Introduction}

Deploying conversational AI in clinical rehabilitation poses a unique infrastructure challenge: separating what patients and clinicians *prefer* from what is clinically *safe to deploy*. Generic annotation platforms assume a single rater population and a single judgment criterion. But clinical AI systems must simultaneously satisfy competing stakeholder demands—end-user engagement and expert safety—without collapsing either signal into the other.

We deployed WebQA, a role-gated, double-blinded evaluation platform, to operationalize this separation. WebQA manages three distinct workflows (Admin, Tester, Rater), enforces server-side response randomization, and exports judgment data as three concurrent products: audit logs for clinical governance, preferred-response training sets for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and chosen-rejected pairs for preference optimization. The system is live around the mRehab Advisory Agent—a voice interface for home rehabilitation deployed on a smartphone-based upper-limb rehabilitation platform previously studied for usability and engagement~\cite{bhattacharjya2019mrehab,langan2020inhome,bhattacharjya2021usability,bo2022progressive,bhattacharjya2023engaging}.

A 9-rater, 200-question pilot yielded 435 complete judgments and exposed a critical operational finding: the configuration rated highest by end-users (mRehab, 53.3\% first-place) was marked safe by domain experts *less* frequently (88.5\%) than lower-preference comparators (94.3\%). This tension—unobservable in single-signal preference frameworks—illustrates why human-in-the-loop clinical AI requires architectural data separation. The platform is designed to detect exactly this kind of misalignment before it reaches training.

distinctions for later filtering and training.

WebQA is the data collection and evaluation layer used around the mRehab
Advisory Agent. It separates rater cohorts, enforces server-side
response blinding with stable per-rater display orders, and exports
flat audit rows, preferred-response supervised fine-tuning (SFT)
examples, and chosen-rejected preference pairs. The output is not only
a benchmark score; it is a reusable alignment dataset with provenance.
toolUseResult.newString:
\section{Introduction}

Deploying conversational AI in clinical rehabilitation poses a unique infrastructure challenge: separating what patients and clinicians *prefer* from what is clinically *safe to deploy*. Generic annotation platforms assume a single rater population and a single judgment criterion. But clinical AI systems must simultaneously satisfy competing stakeholder demands—end-user engagement and expert safety—without collapsing either signal into the other.

We deployed WebQA, a role-gated, double-blinded evaluation platform, to operationalize this separation. WebQA manages three distinct workflows (Admin, Tester, Rater), enforces server-side response randomization, and exports judgment data as three concurrent products: audit logs for clinical governance, preferred-response training sets for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and chosen-rejected pairs for preference optimization. The system is live around the mRehab Advisory Agent—a voice interface for home rehabilitation deployed on a smartphone-based upper-limb rehabilitation platform previously studied for usability and engagement~\cite{bhattacharjya2019mrehab,langan2020inhome,bhattacharjya2021usability,bo2022progressive,bhattacharjya2023engaging}.

A 9-rater, 200-question pilot yielded 435 complete judgments and exposed a critical operational finding: the configuration rated highest by end-users (mRehab, 53.3\% first-place) was marked safe by domain experts *less* frequently (88.5\%) than lower-preference comparators (94.3\%). This tension—unobservable in single-signal preference frameworks—illustrates why human-in-the-loop clinical AI requires architectural data separation. The platform is designed to detect exactly this kind of misalignment before it reaches training.

135	\section{Related Work}
136	
137	Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured human
138	judgment rather than automatic
139	metrics~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. LLM-as-judge
140	pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena scale preference
141	collection but substitute a model judgment for a clinician
142	one~\cite{zheng2023judging}; WebQA instead keeps a human rater in the
143	loop with a role-specific rubric, closer to prior rehabilitation
144	conversational-agent evaluations~\cite{hocking2023design}. Its forced
145	ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable
146	than independent Likert ratings~\cite{KiritchenkoM17bestworst}, and the
147	ranked triples double as preference pairs in the style of RLHF and
148	DPO~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}. WebQA also keeps
149	safety rubrics beside the rank, since preference signals alone can
150	reward persuasive but incorrect
151	answers~\cite{sharma2023towards}, and splits expert and end-user
152	cohorts, since agreement should be assessed within, not across,
153	populations with different expertise~\cite{artstein2008inter}.

file.content:
\section{Related Work}

Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured human
judgment rather than automatic
metrics~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. LLM-as-judge
pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena scale preference
collection but substitute a model judgment for a clinician
one~\cite{zheng2023judging}; WebQA instead keeps a human rater in the
loop with a role-specific rubric, closer to prior rehabilitation
conversational-agent evaluations~\cite{hocking2023design}. Its forced
ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable
than independent Likert ratings~\cite{KiritchenkoM17bestworst}, and the
ranked triples double as preference pairs in the style of RLHF and
DPO~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}. WebQA also keeps
safety rubrics beside the rank, since preference signals alone can
reward persuasive but incorrect
answers~\cite{sharma2023towards}, and splits expert and end-user
cohorts, since agreement should be assessed within, not across,
populations with different expertise~\cite{artstein2008inter}.

input.old_string:
\section{Related Work}

Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured human
judgment rather than automatic
metrics~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. LLM-as-judge
pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena scale preference
collection but substitute a model judgment for a clinician
one~\cite{zheng2023judging}; WebQA instead keeps a human rater in the
loop with a role-specific rubric, closer to prior rehabilitation
conversational-agent evaluations~\cite{hocking2023design}. Its forced
ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable
than independent Likert ratings~\cite{KiritchenkoM17bestworst}, and the
ranked triples double as preference pairs in the style of RLHF and
DPO~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}. WebQA also keeps
safety rubrics beside the rank, since preference signals alone can
reward persuasive but incorrect

\section{Related Work}

Clinical LLM evaluation increasingly relies on structured human
judgment rather than automatic
metrics~\cite{singhal2023large,arora2025healthbench}. LLM-as-judge
pipelines such as MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena scale preference
collection but substitute a model judgment for a clinician
one~\cite{zheng2023judging}; WebQA instead keeps a human rater in the
loop with a role-specific rubric, closer to prior rehabilitation
conversational-agent evaluations~\cite{hocking2023design}. Its forced
ranking follows evidence that comparative judgments are more reliable
than independent Likert ratings~\cite{KiritchenkoM17bestworst}, and the
ranked triples double as preference pairs in the style of RLHF and
DPO~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}. WebQA also keeps
safety rubrics beside the rank, since preference signals alone can
reward persuasive but incorrect
answers~\cite{sharma2023towards}, and splits expert and end-user
cohorts, since agreement should be assessed within, not across,
populations with different expertise~\cite{artstein2008inter}.

lines: A 9-rater, 200-question pilot yielded 435 complete judgments and exposed a critical operational finding: the configuration rated highest by end-users (mRehab, 53.3\% first-place) was marked safe by domain experts *less* frequently (88.5\%) than lower-preference comparators (94.3\%). This tension—unobservable in single-signal preference frameworks—illustrates why human-in-the-loop clinical AI requires architectural data separation. The platform is designed to detect exactly this kind of misalignment before it reaches training.
