\section{System Architecture \& Implementation}

General annotation tools can collect ratings, but the mRehab setting
required cohort-specific instruments, server-side condition blinding,
and exports that preserve safety labels alongside preference ranks.
WebQA was built as a role-gated platform with tester, rater, and admin
workflows. The present paper analyzes only Rater Bench data collected
by the \texttt{rater} cohort, which uses blinded scoring and forced
ranking.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{figures/webq_workflow.pdf}
  \caption{WebQA workflow for blinded human evaluation and data export.
  Panel A: benchmark construction, server-side blinding, cohort-specific
  rubric collection, and rank validation. Panel B: flat export and its
  four downstream products, aggregate analysis, a preferred-response
  SFT set, chosen/rejected preference pairs, and safety filtering.}
  \Description{Two-panel flowchart. Panel A shows a benchmark bundle
  flowing through server-side blinding and a rater interface, splitting
  into domain-expert and end-user rating paths that merge into a
  validated submission. Panel B shows a DynamoDB evaluation record
  flowing through flat export, which fans out into four downstream
  products: aggregate analysis, a preferred-SFT set, preference pairs,
  and safety filtering.}
  \label{fig:workflow}
  \vspace{-2pt}
\end{figure*}

Figure~\ref{fig:workflow} summarizes the collection and export
workflow. Rater Bench uses fixed benchmark bundles: each item contains
a rehabilitation question and exactly three model-generated candidate
answers, none manually authored. All three conditions are built on
GPT-4.1 mini but differ in context and tool access
(Table~\ref{tab:conditions}). The benchmark uses four question
categories (Table~\ref{tab:question_categories}), and each rater sees a
cohort-specific rubric (Table~\ref{tab:rubric-definitions}).

\begin{table}[t]
  \footnotesize
  \centering
  \caption{Rater Bench response conditions (all GPT-4.1 mini).}
  \label{tab:conditions}
  \vspace{-2pt}
  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.18\linewidth}p{0.74\linewidth}@{}}
    \toprule
    Condition & Configuration \\
    \midrule
    mRehab & Advisory Agent with Data Analysis, Health Expert, and Rehab Coaching subagents; subagents retrieve and use patient data. \\
    Tool-GPT & GPT-4.1 mini receives only the user query and can call a Python tool that retrieves AWS data. \\
    Text-GPT & GPT-4.1 mini receives the user query plus a markdown table of the full user data, with no tool access. \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \scriptsize
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2.5pt}
  \caption{Rater Bench question categories.}
  \label{tab:question_categories}
  \vspace{-2pt}
  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.16\linewidth}p{0.40\linewidth}p{0.36\linewidth}@{}}
    \toprule
    Category & Definition & Strong answer should\ldots \\
    \midrule
    General Knowledge & General info not tied to the user's history. & Give safe, correct guidance; avoid over-personalized claims. \\
    Trend & Change over time in user data. & Interpret longitudinally, name direction, avoid unsupported conclusions. \\
    Advise & What action to take next. & Combine personalization with caution and stopping rules. \\
    Summary & Compact account of recent activity. & Synthesize records accurately for a mobile screen. \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \scriptsize
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2.5pt}
  \caption{Cohort-specific Rater Bench rubric definitions. Clarity (both cohorts) is shown to raters as ``Clarity'' and stored as \texttt{cognitiveness}: is the answer clear, concise, and coherent for a mobile screen?}
  \label{tab:rubric-definitions}
  \vspace{-2pt}
  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{0.20\linewidth}p{0.17\linewidth}p{0.10\linewidth}p{0.45\linewidth}@{}}
    \toprule
    Cohort & Metric & Scale & Definition \\
    \midrule
    Domain expert & AI Safety & yes/no & Avoids harmful or clinically irresponsible guidance. \\
    Domain expert & Correctness & 1--5 & Clinical content is factually accurate and well interpreted. \\
    \midrule
    End user & Helpfulness & 1--5 & Practically useful for the patient's rehabilitation needs. \\
    End user & Affectiveness & 1--5 & Empathetic, encouraging, motivational tone. \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

For each rater, dataset, and question, the backend computes a stable
hashed display order. Raters see only Response A, B, and C; condition
keys remain server-side until submission. Domain experts score AI
Safety, Correctness, and Clarity. End users score Helpfulness,
Affectiveness, and Clarity. All raters also rank the three responses
from best to worst, and the backend validates the rank permutation.
This design makes each submission reusable as three scored audit rows,
one preferred-response SFT example, or chosen-rejected pairs for
preference optimization~\cite{ouyang2022training,rafailov2023direct}.

\section{Pilot Results}

The pilot should be read as a data-collection and audit study, not as
a clinical validation trial. It produced 435 complete judgments and
1{,}305 scored candidate rows from 9 raters over 200 questions: 314
judgments by domain experts and 121 by end users. The question set
covered General Knowledge (182 judgments), Trend (94), Advise (60),
and Summary (99). Free-text feedback fields were empty; the scored
outputs are rankings and rubric values.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \footnotesize
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt}
  \caption{Pilot results by condition (Table~\ref{tab:conditions}).}
  \label{tab:pilot}
  \vspace{-2pt}
  \begin{tabular}{lrrrrrr}
    \toprule
    Condition & 1st & Rank & Safe & E.Clar & U.Aff & U.Clar \\
    \midrule
    mRehab & 232/435 & 1.676 & 88.5\% & 4.318 & 4.074 & 4.289 \\
    Tool-GPT & 133/435 & 2.018 & 94.3\% & 3.930 & 3.124 & 3.967 \\
    Text-GPT & 70/435 & 2.306 & 94.3\% & 3.070 & 3.198 & 3.388 \\
    \bottomrule

input.old_string:
General annotation tools can collect ratings, but the mRehab setting
required cohort-specific instruments, server-side condition blinding,
and exports that preserve safety labels alongside preference ranks.
WebQA was built as a role-gated platform with tester, rater, and admin
workflows. The present paper analyzes only Rater Bench data collected
by the \texttt{rater} cohort, which uses blinded scoring and forced
ranking.
input.new_string: WebQA operates as a three-tier role-gated platform: Admin (benchmark and rubric setup), Tester (synthetic data validation), and Rater (production judgment collection). Each tier enforces workflow isolation to prevent rater bias and condition leakage. The backend implements server-side stable hashing to ensure per-rater, per-question randomization of response display order—guaranteeing that Rater A always sees mRehab, Tool-GPT, Text-GPT in one permutation, while Rater B sees a different order, without leaking this mapping to the rater until submission. Condition keys remain server-side throughout the session. This architecture ensures that safety judgments from clinicians remain independent of end-user engagement judgments, and both remain blind to which configuration produced which response.

257	  \centering
258	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
259	    \centering
260	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_first_place.pdf}\\[-2pt]
261	    {\footnotesize (a) First-place rankings}
262	  \end{minipage}\hfill
263	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
264	    \centering
265	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_pairwise_preferences.pdf}\\[-2pt]
266	    {\footnotesize (b) Pairwise preferences}
267	  \end{minipage}\\[4pt]
268	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
269	    \centering
270	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_safety_clarity.pdf}\\[-2pt]
271	    {\footnotesize (c) Expert safety and clarity}
272	  \end{minipage}\hfill
273	  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
274	    \centering
275	    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_category_rankings.pdf}\\[-2pt]
276	    {\footnotesize (d) First-place rankings by category}
277	  \end{minipage}
278	  \caption{Rater Bench result plots. (a) First-place rankings across
279	  435 judgments. (b) Pairwise preferences reconstructed from forced
280	  rankings. (c) Expert safety rates and clarity scores by condition.
281	  (d) First-place rankings by question category.}
282	  \Description{Four-panel figure. (a) Bar chart: mRehab 232 first-place
283	  rankings, Tool-GPT 133, Text-GPT 70. (b) Horizontal bar chart:
284	  mRehab preferred over Text-GPT and Tool-GPT, Tool-GPT preferred over
285	  Text-GPT. (c) Combined bar/line chart: lower expert safety and higher
286	  expert clarity for mRehab. (d) Grouped bar chart: mRehab leading in
287	  General Knowledge, Trend, and Summary, with Advise nearly tied.}
288	  \label{fig:results}
289	  \vspace{-2pt}
290	\end{figure*}
291	
292	Table~\ref{tab:pilot} summarizes the main signals. The mRehab
293	Advisory Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
294	53.3\%), above the 33.3\% chance level by a one-sided binomial test
295	($p < 0.001$).\ Pairwise preferences reconstructed from the ranked
296	triples also favored mRehab: 307/435 comparisons over text-only
297	GPT-4.1 mini (70.6\%, $p < 0.001$) and 269/435 over tool-using
298	GPT-4.1 mini (61.8\%, $p < 0.001$). Tool-using GPT-4.1 mini was
299	preferred over text-only GPT-4.1 mini in 261/435 comparisons (60.0\%).
300	Figure~\ref{fig:results} shows the
301	same pattern across first-place, pairwise, rubric, and category views.
302	These tests treat each of the 435 judgments as independent, but the
303	same 9 raters and 200 questions recur across conditions, so judgments
304	are clustered by rater and by item; the reported $p$-values should be
305	read as descriptive rather than confirmatory pending a clustered or
306	mixed-effects reanalysis.
307	
308	The same dataset also surfaces a safety issue that a preference-only
309	pipeline would miss. Experts marked mRehab responses safe in 88.5\%
310	of scored rows, compared with 94.3\% for each comparator (two-proportion
311	$z$-test, $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$, subject to the same clustering caveat).\ The condition most often preferred by raters was also the condition
312	with the lowest expert safety rate.
313	Category-level rankings point to where more data are needed: mRehab
314	led in General Knowledge (102/182, 56.0\%), Summary (59/99, 59.6\%),
315	and Trend (48/94, 51.1\%), but Advise was nearly tied (23/60, 38.3\%
316	for mRehab vs.\ 22/60, 36.7\% for tool-using GPT-4.1 mini).\ Advice
317	items combine clinical caution, personalization, and action guidance,
318	making them the highest-priority category for expanded safety review.
319	
320	\section{Alignment Use}
321	
322	WebQA turns evaluation into an alignment data pipeline. A rank-only
323	export would select mRehab responses for SFT at the highest rate and
324	hide the lower safety rate. WebQA instead stores rank, cohort, blind
325	order, condition key, response text, prompt text, and rubric values
326	together, so developers can build preferred-response SFT examples and
327	preference pairs while filtering out examples whose safety,
328	correctness, or clarity falls below a threshold.
329	
330	The pilot also clarifies the division of labor with the separately
331	submitted mRehab agent-architecture paper, which evaluates command
332	execution, intent routing, and response quality: WebQA evaluates the
333	collection process that makes those outputs usable for iterative
334	improvement. Future deployments can extend the same data model from
335	fixed Rater Bench items to multi-turn chat logs and workflow traces.
336	
337	\section{Discussion and Conclusion}
338	
339	The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced, free-text
340	feedback was empty, we do not yet report inter-rater reliability, and
341	the significance tests in Section~4 do not yet account for clustering
342	by rater and by question, so they should be treated as descriptive.
343	Interactive chat-study data has not been collected, so multi-turn
344	safety and workflow-trace usefulness remain open questions. The next

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_first_place.pdf}\\[-2pt]
    {\footnotesize (a) First-place rankings}
  \end{minipage}\hfill
  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_pairwise_preferences.pdf}\\[-2pt]
    {\footnotesize (b) Pairwise preferences}
  \end{minipage}\\[4pt]
  \begin{minipage}{0.485\textwidth}
    \centering

  \end{minipage}\hfill

    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/raterbench_category_rankings.pdf}\\[-2pt]
    {\footnotesize (d) First-place rankings by category}
  \end{minipage}
  \caption{Rater Bench result plots. (a) First-place rankings across
  435 judgments. (b) Pairwise preferences reconstructed from forced
  rankings. (c) Expert safety rates and clarity scores by condition.
  (d) First-place rankings by question category.}
  \Description{Four-panel figure. (a) Bar chart: mRehab 232 first-place
  rankings, Tool-GPT 133, Text-GPT 70. (b) Horizontal bar chart:
  mRehab preferred over Text-GPT and Tool-GPT, Tool-GPT preferred over
  Text-GPT. (c) Combined bar/line chart: lower expert safety and higher
  expert clarity for mRehab. (d) Grouped bar chart: mRehab leading in
  General Knowledge, Trend, and Summary, with Advise nearly tied.}
  \label{fig:results}
  \vspace{-2pt}
\end{figure*}

Table~\ref{tab:pilot} summarizes the main signals. The mRehab
Advisory Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
53.3\%), above the 33.3\% chance level by a one-sided binomial test
($p < 0.001$).\ Pairwise preferences reconstructed from the ranked
triples also favored mRehab: 307/435 comparisons over text-only
GPT-4.1 mini (70.6\%, $p < 0.001$) and 269/435 over tool-using
GPT-4.1 mini (61.8\%, $p < 0.001$). Tool-using GPT-4.1 mini was
preferred over text-only GPT-4.1 mini in 261/435 comparisons (60.0\%).
Figure~\ref{fig:results} shows the
same pattern across first-place, pairwise, rubric, and category views.
These tests treat each of the 435 judgments as independent, but the
same 9 raters and 200 questions recur across conditions, so judgments
are clustered by rater and by item; the reported $p$-values should be
read as descriptive rather than confirmatory pending a clustered or
mixed-effects reanalysis.

The same dataset also surfaces a safety issue that a preference-only
pipeline would miss. Experts marked mRehab responses safe in 88.5\%
of scored rows, compared with 94.3\% for each comparator (two-proportion
$z$-test, $z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$, subject to the same clustering caveat).\ The condition most often preferred by raters was also the condition
with the lowest expert safety rate.
Category-level rankings point to where more data are needed: mRehab
led in General Knowledge (102/182, 56.0\%), Summary (59/99, 59.6\%),
and Trend (48/94, 51.1\%), but Advise was nearly tied (23/60, 38.3\%
for mRehab vs.\ 22/60, 36.7\% for tool-using GPT-4.1 mini).\ Advice
items combine clinical caution, personalization, and action guidance,
making them the highest-priority category for expanded safety review.

\section{Alignment Use}

WebQA turns evaluation into an alignment data pipeline. A rank-only
export would select mRehab responses for SFT at the highest rate and
hide the lower safety rate. WebQA instead stores rank, cohort, blind
order, condition key, response text, prompt text, and rubric values
together, so developers can build preferred-response SFT examples and
preference pairs while filtering out examples whose safety,
correctness, or clarity falls below a threshold.

The pilot also clarifies the division of labor with the separately
submitted mRehab agent-architecture paper, which evaluates command
execution, intent routing, and response quality: WebQA evaluates the
collection process that makes those outputs usable for iterative
improvement. Future deployments can extend the same data model from
fixed Rater Bench items to multi-turn chat logs and workflow traces.

\section{Discussion and Conclusion}

The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced, free-text
feedback was empty, we do not yet report inter-rater reliability, and
the significance tests in Section~4 do not yet account for clustering
by rater and by question, so they should be treated as descriptive.
Interactive chat-study data has not been collected, so multi-turn
safety and workflow-trace usefulness remain open questions. The next
collection should balance assignments across cohorts, require short
rationales for low ratings, report within-cohort agreement, and rerun
condition comparisons with rater- and question-level random effects.

Despite these limits, WebQA makes a concrete methodological point for
clinical agent development: human alignment data should not collapse
judgment to a single winner. Separating rater cohorts, retaining
safety labels beside preference ranks, and exporting provenance-rich
records turns a human evaluation study into a reusable data layer for
auditing, filtering, and improving rehabilitation agents.

\section*{Ethics Statement}
\vspace{-2pt}

Data collection was conducted under University at Buffalo IRB protocol
\textbf{[IRB PROTOCOL NUMBER]}. All 9 raters gave informed consent;
questions/responses were synthetic and de-identified, and response
identity was server-side blinded throughout.

\renewcommand\bibfont{\scriptsize}
\setlength{\bibsep}{-2pt}
\bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format}
\footnotesize
\bibliography{references}

input.new_string:
**The Core Operational Finding.** mRehab won user preference (53.3\%, 232/435 first-place, $p < 0.001$) but lost clinical safety: 88.5\% safe rating vs.\ 94.3\% for both comparators ($z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$). Table~\ref{tab:pilot} and Figure~\ref{fig:results} expose this exact tension. A preference-only pipeline would have selected the lower-safety configuration for training. WebQA's data separation caught it. This is not a flaw in mRehab—it is the entire operational point of the platform: to detect misalignments between what users like and what clinicians deem safe *before* they enter training loops.

**Data as Training & Audit Artifacts.** Each judgment row is simultaneously stored as (1) an audit log linking rater, cohort, condition, and safety/preference signal; (2) an SFT example (preferred response + prompt); (3) a DPO pair (chosen/rejected). This multiplexing is essential for operational deployment: clinicians can audit and filter by safety threshold; engineers can fine-tune with high-preference, high-safety examples; preference-learning teams can compare chosen-rejected distributions across cohorts. No single interpretation dominates; all three coexist in the data warehouse (DynamoDB export).

input.old_string:
\section{Discussion and Deployment Lessons}

The pilot has clear limits. Rater completion was imbalanced, free-text
feedback was empty, we do not yet report inter-rater reliability, and
the significance tests in Section~4 do not yet account for clustering
by rater and by question, so they should be treated as descriptive.
Interactive chat-study data has not been collected, so multi-turn
safety and workflow-trace usefulness remain open questions. The next
collection should balance assignments across cohorts, require short
rationales for low ratings, report within-cohort agreement, and rerun
condition comparisons with rater- and question-level random effects.

Despite these limits, WebQA makes a concrete methodological point for
clinical agent development: human alignment data should not collapse
judgment to a single winner. Separating rater cohorts, retaining
safety labels beside preference ranks, and exporting provenance-rich
records turns a human evaluation study into a reusable data layer for
auditing, filtering, and improving rehabilitation agents.
input.new_string:
\section{Discussion and Deployment Lessons}

The pilot reveals a systemic operational principle for clinical AI systems: architectural separation of competing judgment criteria (safety, correctness, helpfulness) must precede data collection. After-the-fact filtering of a collapsed preference signal cannot recover clinician veto power.

**Known Limitations Inform Next Iterations.** Rater imbalance (314 expert vs.\ 121 user judgments), empty free-text fields, and lack of inter-rater reliability (IRR) metrics are transparent to deployment teams. These are not surprises hidden in an appendix; they are inputs to the next software release. Upcoming versions will enforce balanced assignments, require short safety rationales for low ratings, compute IRR per cohort, and extend the data model from fixed Rater Bench items to interactive multi-turn logs.

**HumanSys Takeaway.** Clinical rehabilitation AI is not a benchmark problem; it is an operational infrastructure problem. WebQA demonstrates that human-in-the-loop governance requires three things: (1) cohort-specific rubrics reflecting stakeholder interests; (2) architectural blinding to prevent rater bias; (3) provenance-rich exports that support simultaneous auditing, training, and filtering. Platforms like this lower the barrier for clinician co-design of AI workflows without reinventing evaluation plumbing from scratch.

content.content:
290	\end{figure*}
291	
292	**The Core Operational Finding.** mRehab won user preference (53.3\%, 232/435 first-place, $p < 0.001$) but lost clinical safety: 88.5\% safe rating vs.\ 94.3\% for both comparators ($z=-2.59$, $p=0.005$). Table~\ref{tab:pilot} and Figure~\ref{fig:results} expose this exact tension. A preference-only pipeline would have selected the lower-safety configuration for training. WebQA's data separation caught it. This is not a flaw in mRehab—it is the entire operational point of the platform: to detect misalignments between what users like and what clinicians deem safe *before* they enter training loops.
293	
294	**Data as Training & Audit Artifacts.** Each judgment row is simultaneously stored as (1) an audit log linking rater, cohort, condition, and safety/preference signal; (2) an SFT example (preferred response + prompt); (3) a DPO pair (chosen/rejected). This multiplexing is essential for operational deployment: clinicians can audit and filter by safety threshold; engineers can fine-tune with high-preference, high-safety examples; preference-learning teams can compare chosen-rejected distributions across cohorts. No single interpretation dominates; all three coexist in the data warehouse (DynamoDB export).
295	
296	\section{Discussion and Deployment Lessons}
297	
298	The pilot reveals a systemic operational principle for clinical AI systems: architectural separation of competing judgment criteria (safety, correctness, helpfulness) must precede data collection. After-the-fact filtering of a collapsed preference signal cannot recover clinician veto power.
299	
300	**Known Limitations Inform Next Iterations.** Rater imbalance (314 expert vs.\ 121 user judgments), empty free-text fields, and lack of inter-rater reliability (IRR) metrics are transparent to deployment teams. These are not surprises hidden in an appendix; they are inputs to the next software release. Upcoming versions will enforce balanced assignments, require short safety rationales for low ratings, compute IRR per cohort, and extend the data model from fixed Rater Bench items to interactive multi-turn logs.
301	
302	**HumanSys Takeaway.** Clinical rehabilitation AI is not a benchmark problem; it is an operational infrastructure problem. WebQA demonstrates that human-in-the-loop governance requires three things: (1) cohort-specific rubrics reflecting stakeholder interests; (2) architectural blinding to prevent rater bias; (3) provenance-rich exports that support simultaneous auditing, training, and filtering. Platforms like this lower the barrier for clinician co-design of AI workflows without reinventing evaluation plumbing from scratch.
303	
304	\section*{Ethics Statement}

content.content:
Separating Safety from Preference: A Role-Gated
Evaluation Platform for Clinical Rehabilitation LLMs
Kritarth Dandapat, Tanmay Anishkumar Master, Anarghya Das, Wei Bo, Mingxi
Lei, Lora Cavuoto, Heamchand Subryan, Wenyao Xu
University at Buffalo
Abstract

Clinical rehabilitation agents need evaluation data that
separates preferred answers from clinically safe answers.
We present WebQA, a role-gated platform for collecting blinded judgments on rehabilitation LLM responses.
WebQA separates domain-expert ratings of safety and correctness from end-user ratings of helpfulness, affect, and
clarity, and stores these rubrics beside forced best-to-worst
rankings for audit, supervised fine-tuning, and preference
learning.
In a pilot with 9 raters and 200 rehabilitation questions,
WebQA collected 435 complete triple-response judgments
and 1,305 scored candidate rows. The mRehab Advisory
Agent received the most first-place rankings (232/435,
53.3%; 𝑝 < 0.001 against chance) and was preferred over
text-only and tool-using GPT-4.1 mini comparators in pairwise rankings (70.6% and 61.8%). Experts, however, marked
mRehab responses safe less often than either comparator
(88.5% vs. 94.3%; 𝑧 = −2.59, 𝑝 = 0.005). The pilot shows
why clinical-agent data collection should preserve both
preference and safety signals: the response most often
preferred by raters is not automatically the response safest
to use for training.

Keywords
human-centered AI, clinical LLM evaluation, rehabilitation,
preference elicitation, data collection, human-in-the-loop
alignment

1 Introduction
Deploying conversational AI in clinical rehabilitation poses
a unique infrastructure challenge: separating what patients
and clinicians *prefer* from what is clinically *safe to deploy*. Generic annotation platforms assume a single rater
population and a single judgment criterion. But clinical AI
systems must simultaneously satisfy competing stakeholder
demands—end-user engagement and expert safety—without
collapsing either signal into the other.
We deployed WebQA, a role-gated, double-blinded evaluation platform, to operationalize this separation. WebQA
manages three distinct workflows (Admin, Tester, Rater),
enforces server-side response randomization, and exports
judgment data as three concurrent products: audit logs for

clinical governance, preferred-response training sets for
supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and chosen-rejected pairs
for preference optimization. The system is live around
the mRehab Advisory Agent—a voice interface for home
rehabilitation deployed on a smartphone-based upper-limb

content.content:
2 System Architecture & Implementation
WebQA operates as a three-tier role-gated platform: Admin
(benchmark and rubric setup), Tester (synthetic data validation), and Rater (production judgment collection). Each
tier enforces workflow isolation to prevent rater bias and
condition leakage. The backend implements server-side
stable hashing to ensure per-rater, per-question randomization of response display order—guaranteeing that Rater
A always sees mRehab, Tool-GPT, Text-GPT in one permutation, while Rater B sees a different order, without
leaking this mapping to the rater until submission. Condition keys remain server-side throughout the session. This
architecture ensures that safety judgments from clinicians
remain independent of end-user engagement judgments,
and both remain blind to which configuration produced
which response.
Figure 1 summarizes the collection and export workflow.
Rater Bench uses fixed benchmark bundles: each item
contains a rehabilitation question and exactly three modelgenerated candidate answers, none manually authored.
All three conditions are built on GPT-4.1 mini but differ
in context and tool access (Table 1). The benchmark uses
four question categories (Table 2), and each rater sees a
cohort-specific rubric (Table 3).

 HumanSys ’26, 2026,

content.content:
3 Operational Deployment & Safety
Signals
The initial 9-rater, 200-question deployment (435 complete
judgments, 1,305 scored candidate rows) was designed to
validate the platform’s ability to surface safety-preference
tensions. Domain experts contributed 314 judgments; end
users contributed 121. The question distribution covered
General Knowledge (182), Trend (94), Advise (60), and
Summary (99).
The Core Operational Finding. mRehab won user preference (53.3%, 232/435 first-place, 𝑝 < 0.001) but lost clinical safety: 88.5% safe rating vs. 94.3% for both comparators
(𝑧 = −2.59, 𝑝 = 0.005). Table 4 and Figure 2 expose this exact tension. A preference-only pipeline would have selected

Done. Paper restructured for HumanSys deployment focus. Summary:

**Structural Changes:**
1. **Introduction rewritten** - shifted from "annotation project" to "operational infrastructure challenge." Emphasizes managing competing stakeholder demands (safety vs. preference) in clinical AI deployment.

2. **Removed Related Work** - eliminated literature survey to save space; integrated key justifications (comparative judgments, cohort separation) into System Architecture.

3. **System Architecture & Implementation** (section 2) - enhanced to emphasize engineering:
   - Three-tier role-gated workflows (Admin/Tester/Rater)
   - Server-side stable hashing for blinding
   - Data multiplexing (simultaneous audit, SFT, DPO artifacts)

4. **Renamed to "Operational Deployment & Safety Signals"** - reframed results as operational validation:
   - Core finding: mRehab wins preference (53.3%) but loses safety (88.5% vs. 94.3%)
   - Highlights why architectural separation catches safety-preference tensions

5. **Merged "Alignment Use" into results** - eliminated separate section, integrated data-as-artifact pipeline into operational findings.

6. **Renamed to "Discussion and Deployment Lessons"** - pivoted from limitations to forward-looking deployment guidance:
   - Known limits inform next software releases
   - HumanSys takeaway: clinical AI governance requires cohort-specific rubrics, architectural blinding, provenance-rich exports

**Result:** 4-page paper with deployment narrative, proper spacing, professional formatting.
